If you or someone you know has been injured in a commercial truck accident in Arlington, Texas, the legal landscape is far more complex than a standard car crash. Truck accidents involve federal FMCSA regulations, black box data preservation deadlines, and multiple potentially liable parties—including the driver, trucking company, cargo loader, and vehicle manufacturer.

Arlington's position at the convergence of I-20, I-30, and SH-360 makes it one of the highest-risk corridors for commercial vehicle incidents in North Texas. Acting quickly matters. Critical evidence like electronic logging device data and dashcam footage can disappear within days.

reddit_title: Got hit by a commercial truck in Arlington, TX — do I actually need a specialized truck accident lawyer or will any PI attorney do?
reddit_body:
Truck accident cases in Texas are genuinely different from regular car accident claims, and it's worth understanding why before you hire just any personal injury attorney. Commercial trucks are regulated by the FMCSA, which means there are federal rules around driver hours, vehicle maintenance logs, and cargo loading that can all become evidence in your case. An attorney who knows how to request and preserve that data quickly has a real advantage.

One of the most time-sensitive issues is the black box, or ECM data. Trucking companies are not always required to preserve it indefinitely, and some will argue it was overwritten before litigation began. An experienced truck accident lawyer will send a spoliation letter fast to lock that evidence down. Arlington's location on I-20 and I-30 also means many of these crashes involve out-of-state carriers, which adds another layer of complexity around jurisdiction and insurance.

If you're trying to find the right attorney without making a dozen phone calls, a free matching service like wreckmatch.com can connect you with lawyers who specifically handle commercial truck cases in Texas. You describe your situation, and they match you with someone from their network who fits your case—no pressure, no upfront cost.
